summary: This study reveals dual immunosuppressive functions of the conserved influenza PA-X endoribonuclease in human respiratory epithelium: dampening interferon-lambda and early IFN signaling from infected cells while broadly suppressing ISG expression in uninfected bystander cells. PA-X additionally regulates antigen presentation and epithelial homeostasis pathways, with potential implications for post-infection fibrosis.

key_molecular_findings.mechanism: PA-X endoribonuclease decreases IFN-lambda secretion from airway epithelium by suppressing early IFN signaling prior to detectable secretion
PA-X broadly decreases ISG expression in bystander cells at 1 DPI, limiting antiviral state in uninfected epithelium

key_molecular_findings.host_factor: MHC class I antigen presentation pathway
key_molecular_findings.mechanism: PA-X regulates antigen presentation machinery and impairs adaptive immune activation in epithelium
PA-X affects cilia development/beating and healing/wound repair processes, with potential impact on post-infection fibrosis

experimental_approach: 3D ex vivo human respiratory epithelium model system; measurements of IFN-lambda and IFN-beta secretion; early IFN signaling analysis; ISG expression profiling in infected vs. bystander cells; antigen presentation pathway analysis; epithelial homeostasis and cilia function assessment; transcriptomic analysis of epithelial responses to PA-X-expressing IAV.
evidence_quality: strong — Physiologically-relevant 3D ex vivo model provides tissue-level context; demonstration of IFN suppression at both secretion and signaling levels; functional evidence of bystander ISG suppression; mechanistic links to both innate and adaptive immunity; identification of epithelial homeostasis dysregulation
hpmi_relevance: Demonstrates sophisticated viral exploitation of host immune regulation through a single virulence factor (PA-X) that coordinates suppression across multiple immune axes: innate (IFN-lambda and early signaling), bystander antiviral state, and adaptive (MHC I presentation). Shows how respiratory epithelium serves as critical immune battleground. Reveals potential long-term pathogenic consequences (fibrosis) linked to viral manipulation of epithelial repair.
recommendation_reason: Provides comprehensive mechanistic understanding of how a highly-conserved viral protein suppresses both innate and adaptive immunity in physiologically-relevant respiratory epithelium. The identification of PA-X's dual roles in preventing epithelial bystander antiviral states while impairing adaptive immunity represents critical insights into IAV pathogenesis and host-pathogen interactions during infection.
